title: MHz

infobox.value: (symbol: **Hz**) is the unit of [frequency](/wiki/Frequency "Frequency") in the [International System of Units](/wiki/International_System_of_Units "International System of Units") (SI), often described as being equivalent to one event (or [cycle](/wiki/Cycle_per_second "Cycle per second")) per [second](/wiki/Second "Second").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[a]](#cite_note-3) The hertz is an [SI derived unit](/wiki/SI_derived_unit "SI derived unit") whose formal expression in terms of [SI base units](/wiki/SI_base_unit "SI base unit") is 1/s or s−1, meaning that one hertz is one per second or the [reciprocal of one second](/wiki/Inverse_second "Inverse second").[[2]](#cite_note-SI_Brochure_9-2) It is used only in the case of periodic events. It is named after [Heinrich Rudolf Hertz](/wiki/Heinrich_Hertz "Heinrich Hertz") (1857–1894), the first person to provide conclusive proof of the existence of [electromagnetic waves](/wiki/Electromagnetic_wave "Electromagnetic wave"). For high frequencies, the unit is commonly expressed in [multiples](/wiki/Metric_prefix "Metric prefix"): kilohertz (kHz), megahertz (MHz), gigahertz (GHz), terahertz (THz).

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [MHz](/w/index.php?title=MHz&redirect=no "MHz"))

SI unit of frequency

This article is about the unit measure. For the car rental company, see [Hertz Global Holdings](/wiki/Hertz_Global_Holdings "Hertz Global Holdings"). For the surname, see [Hertz (name)](/wiki/Hertz_(name) "Hertz (name)"). For other uses, see [Hertz (disambiguation)](/wiki/Hertz_(disambiguation) "Hertz (disambiguation)").

"Hz" and "Megahertz" redirect here. For other uses, see [Hz (disambiguation)](/wiki/Hz_(disambiguation) "Hz (disambiguation)") and [Megahertz (disambiguation)](/wiki/Megahertz_(disambiguation) "Megahertz (disambiguation)").

| hertz | |
| --- | --- |
| Top to bottom: Lights flashing at [frequencies](/wiki/Frequency "Frequency") *f* = 0.5 Hz, 1.0 Hz and 2.0 Hz; that is, at 0.5, 1.0 and 2.0 flashes per second, respectively. The time between each flash – the *period* *T* – is given by 1⁄*f* (the [reciprocal](/wiki/Multiplicative_inverse "Multiplicative inverse") of *f* ); that is, 2, 1 and 0.5 seconds, respectively. | |
| General information | |
| [Unit system](/wiki/System_of_units_of_measurement "System of units of measurement") | [SI](/wiki/SI "SI") |
| Unit of | [frequency](/wiki/Frequency "Frequency") |
| Symbol | Hz |
| Named after | [Heinrich Hertz](/wiki/Heinrich_Hertz "Heinrich Hertz") |
| In [SI base units](/wiki/SI_base_unit "SI base unit") | [s−1](/wiki/Inverse_second "Inverse second") |

The **hertz** (symbol: **Hz**) is the unit of [frequency](/wiki/Frequency "Frequency") in the [International System of Units](/wiki/International_System_of_Units "International System of Units") (SI), often described as being equivalent to one event (or [cycle](/wiki/Cycle_per_second "Cycle per second")) per [second](/wiki/Second "Second").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[a]](#cite_note-3) The hertz is an [SI derived unit](/wiki/SI_derived_unit "SI derived unit") whose formal expression in terms of [SI base units](/wiki/SI_base_unit "SI base unit") is 1/s or s−1, meaning that one hertz is one per second or the [reciprocal of one second](/wiki/Inverse_second "Inverse second").[[2]](#cite_note-SI_Brochure_9-2) It is used only in the case of periodic events. It is named after [Heinrich Rudolf Hertz](/wiki/Heinrich_Hertz "Heinrich Hertz") (1857–1894), the first person to provide conclusive proof of the existence of [electromagnetic waves](/wiki/Electromagnetic_wave "Electromagnetic wave"). For high frequencies, the unit is commonly expressed in [multiples](/wiki/Metric_prefix "Metric prefix"): kilohertz (kHz), megahertz (MHz), gigahertz (GHz), terahertz (THz).

Some of the unit's most common uses are in the description of [periodic waveforms](/wiki/Periodic_waveform "Periodic waveform") and [musical tones](/wiki/Musical_tone "Musical tone"), particularly those used in [radio](/wiki/Radio "Radio")- and audio-related applications. It is also used to describe the [clock speeds](/wiki/Clock_speed "Clock speed") at which computers and other electronics are driven. The units are sometimes also used as a representation of the [energy of a photon](/wiki/Photon_energy "Photon energy"), via the [Planck relation](/wiki/Planck_relation "Planck relation") *E* = *hν*, where *E* is the photon's energy, *ν* is its frequency, and *h* is the [Planck constant](/wiki/Planck_constant "Planck constant").

## Definition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hertz&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Definition")]

The hertz is defined as one per second for periodic events. The [International Committee for Weights and Measures](/wiki/International_Committee_for_Weights_and_Measures "International Committee for Weights and Measures") defined the second as "the duration of 9192631770 periods of the radiation corresponding to the transition between the two hyperfine levels of the ground state of the [caesium](/wiki/Caesium "Caesium")-133 atom"[[3]](#cite_note-4)[[4]](#cite_note-5) and then adds: "It follows that the hyperfine splitting in the ground state of the caesium 133 atom is exactly 9192631770 hertz, *ν*hfs Cs = 9192631770 Hz." The dimension of the unit hertz is 1/time (T−1). Expressed in base SI units, the unit is the reciprocal second (1/s).

In English, "hertz" is also used as the plural form.[[5]](#cite_note-6) As an SI unit, Hz can be [prefixed](/wiki/Metric_prefix "Metric prefix"); commonly used multiples are kHz (kilohertz, 103 Hz), MHz (megahertz, 106 Hz), GHz (gigahertz, 109 Hz) and THz (terahertz, 1012 Hz). One hertz (i.e. one per second) simply means "one periodic event occurs per second" (where the event being counted may be a complete cycle); 100 Hz means "one hundred periodic events occur per second", and so on. The unit may be applied to any periodic event—for example, a clock might be said to tick at 1 Hz, or a human heart might be said to [beat](/wiki/Heart_rate "Heart rate") at 1.2 Hz.

The occurrence [rate of aperiodic](/wiki/Aperiodic_frequency "Aperiodic frequency") or [stochastic](/wiki/Stochastic "Stochastic") events is expressed in *[reciprocal second](/wiki/Reciprocal_second "Reciprocal second")* or *inverse second* (1/s or s−1) in general or, in the specific case of [radioactivity](/wiki/Radioactivity "Radioactivity"), in [becquerels](/wiki/Becquerel "Becquerel").[[b]](#cite_note-8) Whereas 1 Hz (one per second) specifically refers to one cycle (or periodic event) per second, 1 Bq (also one per second) specifically refers to one radionuclide event per second on average.

Even though frequency, [angular velocity](/wiki/Angular_velocity "Angular velocity"), [angular frequency](/wiki/Angular_frequency "Angular frequency") and radioactivity all have the dimension T−1, of these only frequency is expressed using the unit hertz.[[7]](#cite_note-9) Thus a disc rotating at 60 revolutions per minute (rpm) is said to have an angular velocity of 2π rad/s and a [frequency of rotation](/wiki/Rotational_frequency "Rotational frequency") of 1 Hz. The correspondence between a frequency *f* with the unit hertz and an angular velocity *ω* with the unit [radians](/wiki/Radians "Radians") per second is

:   ![{\displaystyle \omega =2\pi f}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7a1bf35d395c2d52391265e4bbda0aed14f52579) and ![{\displaystyle f={\frac {\omega }{2\pi }}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/bd2f3b31c4ca5791144e770032f16cb1ad2ebdcf)

The hertz is named after [Heinrich Hertz](/wiki/Heinrich_Hertz "Heinrich Hertz"). As with every [SI](/wiki/International_System_of_Units "International System of Units") unit named after a person, its symbol starts with an [upper case](/wiki/Upper_case "Upper case") letter (Hz), but when written in full, it follows the rules for capitalisation of a [common noun](/wiki/Common_noun "Common noun"); i.e., *hertz* becomes capitalised at the beginning of a sentence and in titles but is otherwise in lower case.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hertz&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Further information: [Cycle per second](/wiki/Cycle_per_second "Cycle per second")

The hertz is named after the German physicist [Heinrich Hertz](/wiki/Heinrich_Hertz "Heinrich Hertz") (1857–1894), who made important scientific contributions to the study of [electromagnetism](/wiki/Electromagnetism "Electromagnetism"). The name was established by the [International Electrotechnical Commission](/wiki/International_Electrotechnical_Commission "International Electrotechnical Commission") (IEC) in 1935.[[8]](#cite_note-10) It was adopted by the [General Conference on Weights and Measures](/wiki/General_Conference_on_Weights_and_Measures "General Conference on Weights and Measures") (CGPM) (*Conférence générale des poids et mesures*) in 1960, replacing the previous name for the unit, "cycles per second" (cps), along with its related multiples, primarily "kilocycles per second" (kc/s) and "megacycles per second" (Mc/s), and occasionally "kilomegacycles per second" (kMc/s). The term "cycles per second" was largely replaced by "hertz" by the 1970s.[[9]](#cite_note-11)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

In some usage, the "per second" was omitted, so that "megacycles" (Mc) was used as an abbreviation of "megacycles per second" (that is, megahertz (MHz)).[[10]](#cite_note-12)

## Applications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hertz&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Applications")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/e/ea/Wave_frequency.gif)](/wiki/File:Wave_frequency.gif)

A [sine wave](/wiki/Sine_wave "Sine wave") with varying frequency

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Wiggers_Diagram.svg/500px-Wiggers_Diagram.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Wiggers_Diagram.svg)

[A heartbeat](/wiki/Cardiac_cycle "Cardiac cycle") is an example of a non-[sinusoidal](/wiki/Sine_wave "Sine wave") periodic phenomenon that may be analyzed in terms of frequency. Two cycles are illustrated.

### Sound and vibration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hertz&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Sound and vibration")]

[Sound](/wiki/Sound "Sound") is a traveling [longitudinal wave](/wiki/Longitudinal_wave "Longitudinal wave"), which is an [oscillation](/wiki/Oscillation "Oscillation") of [pressure](/wiki/Pressure "Pressure"). Humans perceive the frequency of a sound as its [pitch](/wiki/Pitch_(music) "Pitch (music)"). Each [musical note](/wiki/Musical_note "Musical note") corresponds to a particular frequency. An infant's ear is able to perceive frequencies ranging from 20 Hz to 20000 Hz; the average [adult human](/wiki/Adult "Adult") can hear sounds between 20 Hz and 16000 Hz.[[11]](#cite_note-13) The range of [ultrasound](/wiki/Ultrasound "Ultrasound"), [infrasound](/wiki/Infrasound "Infrasound") and other physical vibrations such as [molecular](/wiki/Molecular_vibration "Molecular vibration") and [atomic vibrations](/wiki/Atom_vibrations "Atom vibrations") extends from a few [femtohertz](/wiki/Femto "Femto")[[12]](#cite_note-science_nasa_gov-14) into the [terahertz](/wiki/Tera- "Tera-") range[[c]](#cite_note-15) and beyond.[[12]](#cite_note-science_nasa_gov-14)

### Electromagnetic radiation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hertz&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Electromagnetic radiation")]

[Electromagnetic radiation](/wiki/Electromagnetic_radiation "Electromagnetic radiation") is often described by its frequency—the number of oscillations of the perpendicular electric and magnetic fields per second—expressed in hertz.

Radio frequency radiation is usually measured in kilohertz (kHz), megahertz (MHz), or gigahertz (GHz), with the latter known as [microwaves](/wiki/Microwave "Microwave"). [Light](/wiki/Light "Light") is electromagnetic radiation that is even higher in frequency, and has frequencies in the range of tens of terahertz (THz, [infrared](/wiki/Infrared "Infrared")) to a few petahertz (PHz, [ultraviolet](/wiki/Ultraviolet "Ultraviolet")), with the [visible spectrum](/wiki/Visible_spectrum "Visible spectrum") being 400–790 THz. Electromagnetic radiation with frequencies in the low terahertz range (intermediate between those of the highest normally usable radio frequencies and long-wave infrared light) is often called [terahertz radiation](/wiki/Terahertz_radiation "Terahertz radiation"). Even higher frequencies exist, such as that of [X-rays](/wiki/X-ray "X-ray") and [gamma rays](/wiki/Gamma_ray "Gamma ray"), which can be measured in exahertz (EHz).

For historical reasons, the frequencies of light and higher frequency electromagnetic radiation are more commonly specified in terms of their [wavelengths](/wiki/Wavelength "Wavelength") or [photon energies](/wiki/Photon_energy "Photon energy"): for a more detailed treatment of this and the above frequency ranges, see *[Electromagnetic spectrum](/wiki/Electromagnetic_spectrum "Electromagnetic spectrum")*.

### Gravitational waves

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hertz&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Gravitational waves")]

Observations of [gravitational waves](/wiki/Gravitational_wave "Gravitational wave") are conducted in the 30–7000 Hz range by laser [interferometers](/wiki/Interferometer "Interferometer") like [LIGO](/wiki/LIGO "LIGO"), and the nanohertz (1–1000 nHz) range by [pulsar timing arrays](/wiki/Pulsar_timing_array "Pulsar timing array"). Future space-based detectors are planned to fill in the gap, with [LISA](/wiki/Laser_Interferometer_Space_Antenna "Laser Interferometer Space Antenna") operating from 0.1–10 mHz (with some sensitivity from 10 μHz to 100 mHz), and [DECIGO](/wiki/Deci-hertz_Interferometer_Gravitational_wave_Observatory "Deci-hertz Interferometer Gravitational wave Observatory") in the 0.1–10 Hz range.

### Computers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hertz&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Computers")]

Further information on why frequency is a flawed speed indicator for computers: [Megahertz myth](/wiki/Megahertz_myth "Megahertz myth")

In computers, most [central processing units](/wiki/Central_processing_unit "Central processing unit") (CPU) are labeled in terms of their [clock rate](/wiki/Clock_rate "Clock rate") expressed in megahertz (MHz) or gigahertz (GHz). This specification refers to the frequency of the CPU's master [clock signal](/wiki/Clock_signal "Clock signal"). This signal is nominally a [square wave](/wiki/Square_wave_(waveform) "Square wave (waveform)"), which is an electrical voltage that switches between low and high logic levels at regular intervals. As the hertz has become the primary unit of measurement accepted by the general populace to determine the performance of a CPU, many experts have criticized this approach, which they claim is an [easily manipulable benchmark](/wiki/Megahertz_myth "Megahertz myth"). Some processors use multiple clock cycles to perform a single operation, while others can perform multiple operations in a single cycle.[[13]](#cite_note-16) For personal computers, CPU clock speeds have ranged from approximately 1 MHz in the late 1970s ([Atari](/wiki/Atari "Atari"), [Commodore](/wiki/Commodore_International "Commodore International"), [Apple computers](/wiki/Apple_computers "Apple computers")) to up to 6 GHz in [IBM Power microprocessors](/wiki/IBM_Power_microprocessors "IBM Power microprocessors").

Various [computer buses](/wiki/Bus_(computing) "Bus (computing)"), such as the [front-side bus](/wiki/Front-side_bus "Front-side bus") connecting the CPU and [northbridge](/wiki/Northbridge_(computing) "Northbridge (computing)"), also operate at various frequencies in the megahertz range.

## SI multiples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hertz&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: SI multiples")]

For examples of various multiples, see [Orders of magnitude (frequency)](/wiki/Orders_of_magnitude_(frequency) "Orders of magnitude (frequency)").

SI multiples of hertz (Hz)

| Submultiples | | | Multiples | | |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Value | SI symbol | Name | Value | SI symbol | Name |
| 10−1 Hz | dHz | decihertz | 101 Hz | daHz | decahertz |
| 10−2 Hz | cHz | centihertz | 102 Hz | hHz | hectohertz |
| 10−3 Hz | mHz | millihertz | 103 Hz | **kHz** | **kilohertz** |
| 10−6 Hz | μHz | microhertz | 106 Hz | **MHz** | **megahertz** |
| 10−9 Hz | nHz | nanohertz | 109 Hz | **GHz** | **gigahertz** |
| 10−12 Hz | pHz | picohertz | 1012 Hz | **THz** | **terahertz** |
| 10−15 Hz | fHz | femtohertz | 1015 Hz | PHz | petahertz |
| 10−18 Hz | aHz | attohertz | 1018 Hz | EHz | exahertz |
| 10−21 Hz | zHz | zeptohertz | 1021 Hz | ZHz | zettahertz |
| 10−24 Hz | yHz | yoctohertz | 1024 Hz | YHz | yottahertz |
| 10−27 Hz | rHz | rontohertz | 1027 Hz | RHz | ronnahertz |
| 10−30 Hz | qHz | quectohertz | 1030 Hz | QHz | quettahertz |
| Common prefixed units are in bold. | | | | | |

Higher frequencies than the [International System of Units](/wiki/International_System_of_Units "International System of Units") provides prefixes for are believed to occur naturally in the frequencies of the quantum-mechanical vibrations of massive particles, although these are not directly observable and must be inferred through other phenomena. By convention, these are typically not expressed in hertz, but in terms of the equivalent energy, which is proportional to the frequency by the factor of the [Planck constant](/wiki/Planck_constant "Planck constant").

## Unicode

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hertz&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Unicode")]

The [CJK Compatibility](/wiki/CJK_Compatibility "CJK Compatibility") block in [Unicode](/wiki/Unicode "Unicode") contains characters for common SI units for frequency. These are intended for compatibility with East Asian character encodings, and not for use in new documents (which would be expected to use Latin letters, e.g. "MHz").[[14]](#cite_note-Unicode-U3300-17)

* U+3339 ㌹ SQUARE HERUTU (ヘルツ, *herutsu*)
* U+3390 ㎐ SQUARE HZ (Hz)
* U+3391 ㎑ SQUARE KHZ (kHz)
* U+3392 ㎒ SQUARE MHZ (MHz)
* U+3393 ㎓ SQUARE GHZ (GHz)
* U+3394 ㎔ SQUARE THZ (THz)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hertz&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-3)** Although hertz is often said to imply cycle per second (cps), the SI explicitly states that "cycle" and "cps" are not units in the SI, likely due to ambiguity in the terms.[[2]](#cite_note-SI_Brochure_9-2)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-8)** "(d) The hertz is used only for periodic phenomena, and the becquerel (Bq) is used only for stochastic processes in activity referred to a radionuclide."[[6]](#cite_note-BIPMtable3-7)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-15)** [Atomic vibrations](/wiki/Atom_vibrations "Atom vibrations") are typically on the order of tens of terahertz
